full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When our children were quite young, they attended a preschool at an Episcopal Moravian Church<|13.90|> <|13.90|> in Charlotte, North Carolina called Little Church on the Lane.<|18.76|> <|18.76|> Sounds like something out of a storybook, doesn't it?<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On our drive home through our neighborhood, we often saw a man with some degree of blindness and his dog walking.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> The man had a white tipped cane that he tapped along the sidewalk with his service dog dutifully by his side.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And our oldest child, William, who was then only three years old, named him Bartimaeus.<|7.50|> <|7.50|> I thought that was sweet, and I thought that showed what a great job the preschool and we<|13.72|> <|13.72|> were doing teaching him stories of the Bible.<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I wish I had also taken the opportunity to say a bit more to him about this story,<|10.46|> <|10.46|> pointed out the hope and the legacy of calling this man Bartimaeus.<|17.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This story is one of the more familiar healing stories in the Bible.<|6.84|> <|6.84|> It appears in all three synoptic gospels, and it is one of just a couple of stories out<|14.94|> <|14.94|> of the 30 or so healing stories in the gospels in which the person being healed is named.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bartimaeus, the blind man who sat by the road outside of Jericho on the way to Jerusalem.<|10.20|> <|10.20|> It seems simple on the surface.<|13.84|> <|13.84|> It's a bookend to Jesus' other healing of the blind man just a few chapters ago.<|20.62|> <|20.62|> A beggar receives his sight.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> However, it's not that simple.<|4.64|> <|4.64|> Rarely is, is it?<|7.34|> <|7.34|> There's more than one miracle in this story.<|12.56|> <|12.56|> There is more than one example of blindness,<|16.70|> <|16.70|> and there is more than one miracle of seeing.<|21.64|> <|21.64|> This story is layered.<|25.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus, his disciples, and a large crowd are leaving Jericho when Bartimaeus, upon learning<|10.34|> <|10.34|> who is passing by, calls out to Jesus.<|15.96|> <|15.96|> The crowd sternly ordered the blind beggar to be quiet.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> sternly ordered.<|2.30|> <|3.54|> Instead of doing as you might imagine,<|6.48|> <|7.20|> being quiet as the lowliest of the marginalized,<|10.90|> <|12.56|> Bartimaeus cried out even more loudly.<|18.38|> <|19.46|> It was only when Jesus stopped and acknowledged Bartimaeus<|24.94|> <|24.94|> that the crowd acknowledged him too.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus healed the blindness of the crowd.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> Though Bartimaeus is the literal blind man in the story, it's the crowd, the blind man's peers, his culture, his society, that render him unseen.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To their seeing eyes, the blind man by the roadside is invisible.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> His shouts and cries are not worthy of attention.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> His suffering is not important enough for him to warrant a voice.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When the invisible one dares to speak out, the only acceptable thing for the crowd to do is to shush him.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> The only priority of the crowd is to restore order, reestablish the social hierarchy, and maintain the status quo.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus changes this dynamic.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Once the crowd sees Bartimaeus, they can't unsee him.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> Once Jesus opens their eyes to his full humanity, they respond with compassion.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> Take heart. Get up. He is calling to you.<|27.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Perhaps Jesus heals the crowd first so that they can in turn participate in Bartimaeus' healing.<|13.50|> <|13.50|> What the blind man needs is not sight alone.<|20.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Like any other person, he also needs visibility and validation within his own community.<|10.88|> <|12.24|> Jesus grants him both.<|14.16|> <|15.70|> Jesus asks Bartimaeus the same question he asked James and John right before this story.<|24.04|> <|24.04|> We heard it in last week's lectionary.<|27.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What do you want me to do for you?<|3.70|> <|3.70|> Unlike the sons of Zebedee, James and John,<|8.40|> <|8.40|> who wanted prestige,<|10.44|> <|10.44|> they wanted seats at Jesus' left and right side,<|14.88|> <|14.88|> the blind man simply wants mercy.<|20.22|> <|20.22|> And what does this mercy look like?<|23.62|> <|23.62|> It is being able to see mercy.<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> again. Again. Restoring something Bartimaeus once had and restoring him to a society that is equipped to deal with him because it is clearly not equipped to deal with a person suffering with a disability.<|22.30|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and with it a place back in that society.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> The ability to work and not to beg.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> The ability for him to care more fully for himself.<|18.00|> <|18.00|> The ability to see sunsets in the faces of loved ones again.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A reinstatement to that is in itself a healing miracle.<|7.26|> <|8.34|> It is also miraculous, isn't it, that Bartimaeus, in his blindness, sees what the crowd does not.<|17.84|> <|18.70|> He calls Jesus, Son of David, and he calls Jesus, Son of David.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A title that Jesus has not used yet during His public ministry.<|8.52|> <|8.52|> This pronouncement of Jesus as the Messiah, the Son of David, is a pivotal element of<|16.84|> <|16.84|> this final healing story in the Gospel of Mark.<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In this story, as Jesus sets his face towards Jerusalem and all that is yet to come,<|10.50|> <|10.50|> Bartimaeus sees what others cannot.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> In this story, Jesus is truly seen as well.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Once his sight is restored, Bartimaeus throws off his cloak, that cloak which is his mat<|9.86|> <|9.86|> by day and his cover by night, that cloak that may have been his only possession and<|18.62|> <|18.62|> a symbol of his identity as the other, a beggar by the side of the road.<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> no longer content to be sitting by the side of the road,<|5.68|> <|6.18|> he now steps onto that road and follows Jesus on the way,<|12.16|> <|13.00|> joining the disciples and the crowds,<|15.60|> <|16.12|> walking with Jesus as he heads to Jerusalem.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> Amen.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is much, my friends, to ponder in this concise but rich story.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> All the ways of not seeing, all the ways of seeing, all of the ways of being excluded,<|22.00|> <|22.00|> and all of the ways of truly being welcomed.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> in the power of asking and listening.<|5.36|> <|6.80|> Jesus asked Bartimaeus to articulate his heart's desire.<|12.62|> <|13.48|> What do you want me to do for you?<|16.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus asks. He doesn't presume. He doesn't reduce Bartimaeus to his blindness.<|7.56|> <|8.88|> Instead, he honors the fullness and complexity of a real human being<|14.92|> <|14.92|> who likely has many desires, many longings, and many needs.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In asking the question, Jesus invites Bartimaeus into honest self-reflection.<|9.76|> <|9.76|> "What do you want me to do for you?"<|15.00|> <|15.00|> It is at once a beautiful and terrifying question that Jesus asks not only Bartimaeus,<|23.28|> <|23.28|> but of each of us as well.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.34|> Could you answer?<|1.80|> <|4.10|> We should also be asking each other this very question.<|9.58|> <|10.68|> We who share community, we who worship here and in St. Martin's Chapel together<|17.08|> <|17.08|> and share fellowship as we do at coffee hour, as we do in our small groups,<|23.82|> <|23.82|> as we did last Wednesday,<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and will for more weeks to come on Cathedral Night.<|3.84|> <|6.04|> We ask it of each other in here,<|9.20|> <|10.34|> and we ask it of those we find literally<|14.44|> <|14.44|> and figuratively by the side of the road.<|17.46|> <|18.78|> It is how we honor the dignity of every person<|23.52|> <|24.56|> within our walls and outside of them.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is humanizing to ask and not assume, to ask and then listen to the response just as<|11.08|> <|11.08|> Jesus modeled for us with Bartimaeus.<|17.60|> <|17.60|> What do you want me to do for you?<|23.10|>
